coredata.dc:title: Comparative evaluation of microstructure and electrochemical, high-temperature corrosion rates of titanium- and aluminum-modified black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shtefan, Viktoriia
Kanunnikova, Nadiia
Zuyok, Valeriy
coredata.dc:description: A comparative assessment of the microstructure and the rate of electrochemical high-temperature corrosion of black chromium coatings modified with titanium and aluminum, applied to AISI 304 stainless steel, has been conducted. Elemental analysis confirmed the presence of oxygen, chromium, titanium, and aluminum in the coatings. The EDX element mapping method demonstrated that modifying the coating with aluminum oxide positively affects the distribution of elements and reduces the number of cracks. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis revealed that the coatings mainly consist of chromium, with the addition of TiO1.04 and Al2O3 phases, which enhances the coating's resistance. This study presents potentiodynamic polarization curves illustrating the current-voltage dependencies of steel with Cr·CrOx, Cr·CrOx ·TiOy, and Cr·CrOx ·AlOz coatings, highlighting their electrochemical behavior and enhanced protective properties against pitting corrosion. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis reveals the complex equivalent circuit models for these coatings, showing significant increases in impedance and resistance values, particularly for aluminum oxide-modified coatings, which exhibit corrosion resistance. The effectiveness of protective coatings on steel operating as part of a galvanic pair with a zinc anode in a 6 wt% sodium chloride solution was evaluated by measuring the currents in the electrochemical pair, corrosion rates, and the protective influence of zinc in the Zn – AISI 304/coating galvanic pair. The results demonstrated a significant reduction in contact corrosion currents when the coating was modified with titanium or aluminum oxides, reducing the current density by 6–7.5 times after 5 days of exposure. The appearance of the modified coatings remained unchanged even after prolonged testing, highlighting their high level of protective properties. High-temperature corrosion tests at temperatures up to 800 °C revealed that coatings modified with titanium or aluminum oxides exhibit lower weight gain compared to uncoated samples, emphasizing the superior protective properties of these coatings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stainless steel
Black chromium coating
Impedance
X-ray phase analysis
Electrolysis
Corrosion resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of coatings via electrodeposition and sample processing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Appearance of AISI 304 stainless steel a) without coating on plates and M4 screws: b) Cr·CrOx, c) Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and d) Cr·CrOx·AlOz.
SEM images of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel: a) Cr·CrOx, b) Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and c) Cr·CrOx·AlOz.

SEM image of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel with the corresponding EDX line scans (shown in Fig. 4) as well as EDX maps of element distributions EDX maps of the a) Cr·CrOx, b) Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and c) Cr·CrOx·AlOz.
EDX line scan for a) Cr·CrOx, b) Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and c) Cr·CrOx·AlOz coatings (shown in Fig. 3).
XRD patterns of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel before and after long-term corrosion tests.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves in 3.5 wt% NaCl of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel.
Nyquist and Bode plots of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution: a,b) uncoated steel, c,d) Cr·CrOx, e,f) Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and g,h) Cr·CrOx·AlOz.
Electrical equivalent circuits for EIS of the black chromium coatings on the AISI 304 steel.
SEM image of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel a,b,c) after contact corrosion test for another 5 days.
Corrosion kinetics of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel in а) water vapor at 800 °C temperature and in b) logarithmic coordinates.
Dependence of change in mass of the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel on the test temperature in water vapor medium.
Electrolyte composition and modes of coating formation on the AISI 304 steel.

row.entry: Components (make, purity) and modes of forming coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Elemental composition (in wt%) for the black chromium coatings on AISI 304 steel.
Electrochemical parameters decided by EIS method in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature exemplified as shown in Fig. 8.
Values of contact corrosion currents of the galvanic couple with zinc (i gc) of raw steel, AISI 304 steel with the black chromium coatings in 6 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature for 5 days.

head.ce:title: Comparative evaluation of microstructure and electrochemical, high-temperature corrosion rates of titanium- and aluminum-modified black chromium coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Leibniz-Institute for Solid State and Materials Research (IFW)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A comparative assessment of the microstructure and the rate of electrochemical high-temperature corrosion of black chromium coatings modified with titanium and aluminum, applied to AISI 304 stainless steel, has been conducted. Elemental analysis confirmed the presence of oxygen, chromium, titanium, and aluminum in the coatings. The EDX element mapping method demonstrated that modifying the coating with aluminum oxide positively affects the distribution of elements and reduces the number of cracks. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis revealed that the coatings mainly consist of chromium, with the addition of TiO1.04 and Al2O3 phases, which enhances the coating's resistance. This study presents potentiodynamic polarization curves illustrating the current-voltage dependencies of steel with Cr·CrOx, Cr·CrOx ·TiOy, and Cr·CrOx ·AlOz coatings, highlighting their electrochemical behavior and enhanced protective properties against pitting corrosion. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis reveals the complex equivalent circuit models for these coatings, showing significant increases in impedance and resistance values, particularly for aluminum oxide-modified coatings, which exhibit corrosion resistance. The effectiveness of protective coatings on steel operating as part of a galvanic pair with a zinc anode in a 6 wt% sodium chloride solution was evaluated by measuring the currents in the electrochemical pair, corrosion rates, and the protective influence of zinc in the Zn – AISI 304/coating galvanic pair. The results demonstrated a significant reduction in contact corrosion currents when the coating was modified with titanium or aluminum oxides, reducing the current density by 6–7.5 times after 5 days of exposure. The appearance of the modified coatings remained unchanged even after prolonged testing, highlighting their high level of protective properties. High-temperature corrosion tests at temperatures up to 800 °C revealed that coatings modified with titanium or aluminum oxides exhibit lower weight gain compared to uncoated samples, emphasizing the superior protective properties of these coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, there has been a notable focus on studying the corrosion resistance of chromium‑nickel steels in corrosive environments containing chlorides, bromides, etc. [1–5]. These steels are the primary construction materials for most thermal engineering equipment and the structural elements of the active zones of water-water reactors (WWER-1000, WWER-440). AISI 304 steel ensures maximum neutron flux and contact with the high-parameter primary circuit coolant (350 °C, 16.5 MPa). The activation of the material under neutron irradiation and the transfer of radioactive corrosion products into the coolant pose the challenge of finding ways to reduce both the corrosion rate and the dissolution coefficient of the surface layers of oxide films. The long-term experience of using these steels in nuclear power units has revealed their susceptibility to intergranular, fatigue, and uniform corrosion [6–11]. These phenomena negatively affect the safety and operational life of equipment and nuclear fuel in atomic reactors, especially in emergency modes associated with overheating of the primary and secondary circuit coolant. The fastest possible solution to ensure increased corrosion and wear resistance, as well as reduced hydrogen permeability, is the development of protective coatings on steels that, in some form, include chromium [12–15].

Localized corrosion, particularly pitting and crevice corrosion, is a significant concern for stainless steels like AISI 304 when exposed to environments containing chlorides. Chloride ions are highly aggressive and can penetrate the passive oxide layer that typically protects stainless steel. This leads to the initiation of localized corrosion sites where the passive film is compromised [5,9]. The mechanism involves the adsorption of chloride ions onto the surface, destabilizing the passive film and facilitating anodic dissolution at specific points. Once initiated, the localized corrosion process accelerates due to the formation of differential aeration cells and the autocatalytic nature of pit propagation.

In nuclear power applications, the high temperatures and pressures of coolant environments exacerbate these effects, leading to a higher risk of pitting corrosion and material degradation. The susceptibility of chromium-based protective coatings to cracking further amplifies the corrosion rate, as the cracks act as pathways for aggressive ions [8,15]. Thus, developing crack-free, modified coatings with enhanced resistance to chloride ions is essential to improve the operational life and safety of equipment. The addition of aluminum and titanium oxides into chromium coatings is promising, as these oxides can form dense, protective films that reduce crack formation and enhance barrier properties.

Electrochemical deposition of crack-free chromium is an important direction because traditional chromium coatings often suffer from corrosion caused by the presence of cracks rather than wear. To create such coatings, it is necessary to increase the temperature of the electroplating bath above 70 °C, which, although it may reduce hardness, significantly improves the corrosion resistance of the coating, increasing it by 10 times compared to conventional hard chromium coatings. Nevertheless, creating a coating that is both hard and crack-free has long been considered impossible [16–19]. The inclusion of particles such as WC [9], SiC [10], Al2O3 [10], and MoS2 [8] in the metallic matrix, which demonstrate significant improvements in the corrosion and wear resistance of chromium coatings, is currently impossible to achieve by electrochemical means [20–28]. Therefore, the application of chromium coatings deposited from electrolytes with hexavalent chromium, despite its toxicity and carcinogenic properties, continues to be in demand in various industries due to its unique characteristics and advantages [13,29–38]. Thus, research in the field of improving methods for crack-free chromium electroplating remains relevant, offering new solutions to enhance the operational characteristics of various materials.

In our previous works, we modified iron oxide coatings on AISI 304 stainless steel with titanium [20,21,37], aluminum [20,21], and molybdenum compounds [5,20]. The modification of these coatings with these compounds positively affected their protective properties, leading to reduced electrical conductivity and a less defective structure [22–37]. These findings were further confirmed by corrosion and microscopic studies. Thus, we have demonstrated that the modification of metal oxide coatings with valve metal oxides is a promising direction in creating highly effective protective coatings. Therefore, improving the electrolyte composition for obtaining coatings by integrating titanium oxide (TiO2) and aluminum oxide (Al2O3) into black chromium coatings will allow the creation of new materials that increase the durability of steel structures in aggressive environments, thereby eliminating emergency modes at nuclear power plants associated with overheating of the primary and secondary circuit coolant.

The objective of this scientific study is to investigate the impact of modifying black chromium with titanium or aluminum oxides on the protective properties of coatings. The evaluation will be conducted using various methods, including scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-Ray Analysis (EDX), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and others.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of coatings via electrodeposition and sample processing
ce:section.ce:para: AISI 304 steel, characterized by its composition (in wt%): C – 0.08, Cr – 19.14, Ni – 10.73, Si – 0.58, Mn – 0.19, Fe – 69.28 (Italinox Industry, Ukraine), was selected as the cathode material. The steel was prepared as rectangular sheets (70 × 10 × 1 mm) for the deposition of black chromium coatings. A platinum sheet served as the anode. Before the electroplating process, the steel samples (or M4 screws from Shandong Nuozhong Steel Co., Ltd., China) were thoroughly cleaned with absolute ethanol and then air-dried for 30 min. The electrodeposition of black chromium coatings was performed in a 200 mL electrochemical cell under constant convection to ensure uniform deposition. Details of the electrolyte compositions and the corresponding process parameters are provided in Table 1 . After deposition, the samples were removed from the electrolyte, thoroughly rinsed, and rapidly dried with high-purity nitrogen [22–25]. The thickness of the black chromium coatings was measured using the MT AKASCAN 20.07 coating thickness gauge.
Three samples of each electrolyte were coated onto AISI 304 steel sheets for each of the following tests.

After electrolysis, the surface morphology and elemental composition of the obtained coatings were examined using a ZEISS EVO 40XVP scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an INCA Energy 350 microanalysis system manufactured by Carl Zeiss (Germany) and Oxford Instruments (England). The SEM captured images by registering secondary electrons through scanning the electron beam over the surface, excited by irradiation with an electron beam at an energy of 15–20 keV. The obtained images were processed using the SMARTSEM software environment [15–17]. Additionally, the phase composition of the coatings was analyzed using a DRON-3.0 X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with a Cu Kα target and a scanning rate of 0.02°/s. The identification of phase compositions was based on the JCPDS card index [5,18]. The crystallite sizes for the Al- and Ti-modified coatings were calculated using the well-known Scherrer [14] equation based on the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the diffraction peaks.

Corrosion resistance studies of the obtained coatings began with cleaning the samples using absolute ethanol, followed by air storage for 3 h. Open circuit potential measurements were then conducted for 30 min to ensure process stabilization. Voltammograms were recorded in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature using a Solartron analytical system from AMETEK with a potential sweep rate of 1 mV·s−1. The research utilized a three-electrode Teflon electrochemical cell, where a saturated calomel electrode (SCE; Hg/Hg2Cl2; ESCE = 241 mV versus SHE) served as the reference electrode, and a platinum ring functioned as the counter electrode.

The tests were conducted at room temperature in a custom-built Teflon cell, which had a bottom hole for a working electrode with a diameter of 5 mm on one side of the sheet. Anodic Tafel slope (b a), cathodic Tafel slope (b c), corrosion current density (i corr), corrosion potential (E corr), pitting potential (E pit) were determined through graphical extrapolation.

To study the structure of the obtained coatings, the method of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at room temperature in the same electrochemical cell. Impedance measurements were conducted using Solartron analytical equipment from AMETEK, in the frequency range from 10−3 to 104 Hz, with an applied AC signal of 10 mV [26–30].

The impedance constant phase element (Z CPE) was defined as follows [31]:
Z CPE
ω
=
1
Y 0
jω
n
where j =
− 1
, Y
0 and n are frequency independent coefficients, n is the exponent related to the uniformity of the surface (0 < n < 1), ω is the angular frequency.

The effective capacitance C eff can be associated with the CPE by the following equation by Brug et al. [39]:
C eff
=
Y 0
1 / n
R f
1 − n
/ n
where R f is film resistance.

The thickness of the passive film can be calculated from the capacitance value using the equation provided in [40]:
C eff
=
ε
ε 0
d eff
where d eff is the thickness of the passive film and ε is the dielectric constant, 15.6 for stainless steel passive film [41] and ε 0 is the permittivity of vacuum (8.8542 × 10−14 F/cm).

The following fitting of the electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) spectra was performed in the frequency range from 10-3 to 104 Hz using ZVIEW 4 software from AMETEK (Scribner, Southern Pines, USA). The EECs were chosen based on an analysis of the coating composition and morphology. Additionally, the chi-squared (χ2) value, which represents the square of the standard deviation between the original data and the calculated spectrum, was determined. All χ2 values of the fitted parameters were around 10−3 ≤ χ2 ≤ 10−4, indicating the reliability of the calculated impedance parameters.

Long-term tests were conducted to assess the protective effectiveness of coatings on steel while operating in a galvanic couple with zinc. The currents were measured using a UNI-T UT15B PRO digital multimeter from BRYMEN. Electrodes forming the contact pairs were immersed in a 500 mL bath filled with a 6 wt% NaCl solution, maintaining the temperature at room temperature for a period of 5 days. The electrodes were electrically connected at one end and partially insulated with Teflon tape, exposing an area of 1.5 cm2. The areas of the anode and cathode were maintained in a 1:1 ratio. Throughout the corrosion tests, the solution capacity exceeded 50 mL·cm−2.
All electrochemical measurements were conducted at room temperature and repeated a minimum of three times to ensure reproducibility.

High-temperature corrosion tests were conducted in a water vapor environment, introduced into a tube furnace at atmospheric pressure within a temperature range of 400 °C to 1000 °C. Distilled water, heated to its boiling point, was employed for steam generation. Prior to entering the tube furnace, the steam underwent an additional heating step to reach a temperature of 250–300 °C, achieved by incorporating an auxiliary heater into a specific section of the steam pipeline. The sheet sample, fully coated on all sides and fabricated to the required geometric dimensions, underwent heating at a rate of approximately 20 °C/s. To assess corrosion resistance, the increase in sample mass (Δm) relative to the sample's surface area (S) was monitored, determined by precision weighing with an error margin of ±0.01 mg. This can be expressed as:
∆ m
S
=
m i
−
m 0
S
where
∆ m
S
represents the change in the mass of the test sample in mg/dm2; S is the surface area of the test sample in dm2;
m 0
represents the initial mass of the sample before testing in mg; while m i denotes the sample mass at i-th stage of the test in mg.
It is important to note that each test for each sample was recorded at least three times to assess the reproducibility of the measurements.

In this study, coatings were deposited using chromium-containing electrolytes modified with titanium or aluminum oxides. The coatings were applied to AISI 304 steel, as depicted in Fig. 1a, not only on flat plates but also on intricately shaped details, such as M4 screws. The resulting coatings exhibited a matte black color (shown in Fig. 1b-c), covering evenly (Fig. S1) the entire surface of both the plate and the screws, as confirmed by electron microscopic images (shown in Fig. 2a-c). The appearance of the coatings remained unchanged by the modifying components. Notably, glare is visible in Fig. 1b-c, which depicts the screws after undergoing electrolysis. All black chromium coatings were obtained with a thickness of 12 ± 1.2 μm.

In this study, coatings were deposited using chromium-containing electrolytes modified with titanium or aluminum oxides. The coatings were applied to AISI 304 steel, as depicted in Fig. 1a, not only on flat plates but also on intricately shaped details, such as M4 screws. The resulting coatings exhibited a matte black color (shown in Fig. 1b-c), covering evenly (Fig. S1) the entire surface of both the plate and the screws, as confirmed by electron microscopic images (shown in Fig. 2a-c). The appearance of the coatings remained unchanged by the modifying components. Notably, glare is visible in Fig. 1b-c, which depicts the screws after undergoing electrolysis. All black chromium coatings were obtained with a thickness of 12 ± 1.2 μm.

Possible reactions of electrochemical formation of black chromium coatings Cr·CrOx, Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and Cr·CrOx·AlOz can be represented in a generalized scheme:
Unlabelled Image

As is known, at pH > 4, chromium exists in the ionic form CrO4 2− and is a very strong oxidizing agent [15], making the formation of chromium oxides in various oxidation states possible. During the electrochemical deposition process, chromium ions are reduced at the cathode, forming metallic chromium [14].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to study the surface morphology of the black chromium coatings. The microphotographs reveal differences in the width and number of cracks between the surfaces of the modified coatings with titanium oxides (shown in Fig. 2b) or aluminum oxides (shown in Fig. 2c) and the coating with black chromium Cr·CrOx (shown in Fig. 2a). The width of cracks for Cr·CrOx coatings is 1.1–1.3 ± 0.1 μm, whereas for coatings modified with titanium oxide, it is 0.6–0.8 ± 0.1 μm. Morphological studies of coatings modified with aluminum oxide indicate that the cracks have the smallest size, measuring 0.1–0.3 ± 0.1 μm. Therefore, it can be assumed that these coatings will exhibit higher anticorrosion properties compared to the rest of the obtained black chromium coatings.

Relief irregularities are observed on the surface of black chromium coatings, presenting as protrusions in the form of pyramids and petals with sizes of 100–150 ± 20 nm (shown in Fig. 2a), 300–600 ± 100 nm (shown in Fig. 2b), and 100–300 ± 50 nm (shown in Fig. 2c) with different orientations. Moreover, the structure of the resulting coatings with black chromium is more continuous than the structure shown by the authors in [9], as depicted in their Figs. 2 and 3 . The observed increase in particle size in black chromium coatings upon the addition of TiO₂ and Al₂O₃ can be attributed to several factors related to the electrochemical deposition process and the properties of the oxide particles. The inclusion of TiO₂ and Al₂O₃ particles in the electrolyte introduces nucleation sites that facilitate the growth of larger structural features. These oxides promote the aggregation of chromium-based phases during deposition, leading to the formation of more pronounced protrusions such as pyramids and petals, as depicted in Fig. 2b and c.

Moreover, the chemical characteristics of these oxides significantly influence the morphology. Al₂O₃, being a high-surface-energy material, enhances the deposition rate locally, resulting in a dense and continuous coating with relatively larger particle sizes compared to coatings without any modifications. Similarly, the inclusion of TiO₂ contributes to an increased particle size due to its catalytic effect on the reduction reactions occurring at the cathode surface, which enhances the localized deposition of chromium compounds.

SEM confirmed the presence of elements such as oxygen, chromium, titanium, and aluminum in the formed coatings (shown in Table 2 ). The significant concentrations of oxygen and chromium indicate the presence of oxygen-containing chromium compounds in the resulting coatings. Notably, when coatings are modified with aluminum and titanium oxides, there is minimal alteration in the concentrations of chromium and oxygen, as well as their ratio.

To assess the uniformity of element distribution, EDX elemental mapping was employed for the black chromium coatings, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The EDX elemental mapping reveals an absence of cracks in the spatial distribution images of Al and Ti. This is in contrast to Cr and O in Cr·CrOx and Cr·CrOx·TiOy coatings (shown in Fig. 3a-c), where cracks are evident. Notably, the spatial distribution of Cr (shown in Fig. 3c) exhibits no cracks when the coating is modified with aluminum oxide. This suggests a positive impact of aluminum oxide on the distribution of other elements in the coating.

EDX line scan analysis, as depicted in Fig. 4a-c, suggests that the coatings are multi-layered, warranting further investigation through the EIS method. Both the lines corresponding to Cr and O exhibit a single minimum, indicating the presence of a crack (shown in Fig. 4a). It is noteworthy that Cr·CrOx·AlOz coatings do not show cracks, suggesting an enhancement in the protective properties of the coating. In general, these studies clearly demonstrate that the addition of alumina or titanium to the electrolyte significantly reduces the crack width of the coating, with the modification using aluminum having a more positive effect.

Analysis of the XRD patterns of the black chromium coatings revealed that the coatings primarily consist of chromium (PCPDFWIN #19-0323, #06-0694 cards), along with phases TiO1.04 (PCPDFWIN #43-1296 card) and Al2O3 (PCPDFWIN #47-1292, #43-1484 cards) (shown in Fig. 5 ). All the intense lines for chromium oxide phases CrO (PCPDFWIN #08-0254 card), CrO2 (PCPDFWIN #43-1040 card), Cr2O3 (PCPDFWIN #38-1479 card), C3O8 (PCPDFWIN #36-1330 card), Cr3O4 (PCPDFWIN #12-0559 card), CrO3 (PCPDFWIN #32-0285 card), Cr2O5 (PCPDFWIN #36-1329 card) are located in the range of 15 to 35 degrees. Therefore, it can be assumed that these oxides are in a finely dispersed state, as evidenced by the background elevation in this region, the color of the obtained coatings as well as the results of EDX analysis. Titanium oxide has a high electron density and a high X-ray absorption coefficient, which leads to a decrease in the intensity of chromium peaks. The presence of titanium and aluminum oxides in the respective phases is expected to enhance the coating's resistance, as these elements are representatives of valve metals, which are known [16] to have high protective properties. XRD diffractograms for Al-modified coatings exhibit sharp and intense peaks corresponding to the crystallographic planes (110), (200), and (113). These peaks indicate a high degree of crystallinity, characterized by an ordered structure with larger crystallites of approximately 9.39 nm and a minimal number of defects. The high crystallinity suggests dense atomic packing, which significantly reduces crack formation and improves the barrier properties of the coating. Additionally, the regular crystalline structure promotes the formation of a stable passivating layer that prevents the penetration of corrosive agents such as water and chloride ions, making Al-modified coatings particularly effective in environments requiring enhanced corrosion resistance. In contrast, Ti-modified coatings display broader diffraction peaks (especially for the (110) and (200) planes), indicating a lower degree of crystallinity, with smaller crystallites of approximately 6.5 nm. The smaller crystallite size and broader peaks may result from the presence of amorphous phases or increased defect density, leading to reduced atomic packing density. This structure could increase the permeability of the coating and diminish its ability to effectively prevent corrosion processes. Based on these findings, it can be expected that Ti-modified coatings will exhibit less pronounced barrier properties and lower corrosion resistance compared to the highly crystalline Al-modified coatings.

Thus, the modification of coatings with titanium or aluminum oxides does not significantly affect the elemental composition of the coating; however, it alters the phase composition, thereby changing the coating's structure. In addition, under conditions where electrolyte penetrates through coating defects, chromium oxide such as CrO₃, as detected by XRD analysis, can promote the passivation of stainless steel [48], which contributes to the corrosion resistance of the steel/coating system.

Fig. 6 illustrates the current-voltage dependencies of steel with coatings formed in various electrolytes (shown in Table 1). Upon reaching a potential of −330 mV, the passive film of the AISI 304 stainless steel is disrupted, leading to the formation of stable pits (Fig. S2). It should be noted that in the potential range from 0.2 V to the pitting potential, current density oscillations are observed, which are caused by successive processes of activation and subsequent passivation. This indicates the formation of metastable pitting on the steel surface [42,43]. Within the transpassivation region, reaching a potential of 200 mV, stainless steel shifts to an active state, leading to the generation of higher valence ions. The anode and cathode branches of the curves obtained for steel with black chromium coatings of various modifications exhibit a high degree of similarity. The black chromium coatings increase the passivity region by 565–667 ± 5 mV, indicating enhanced protective properties against pitting corrosion. Lowest passivity current density j pass = (1.34 ± 0.14)·10−6 A·cm−2 at a potential of 0 V has vs. SCE a coating modified with aluminum oxide. In this case, for Cr·CrOx and Cr·CrOx·TiOy coatings, the passivity current density has close values (3.89 ± 0.21)·10−6 and (5.49 ± 0.26)·10−6 A·cm−2 respectively. Apparently, this fact is due to the defectiveness of the coating, namely the presence or absence of cracks. As it was shown in Chapter 3.2. the Cr·CrOx·AlOz coating does not have cracks and therefore demonstrates higher protective properties.

Fig. 6 illustrates the current-voltage dependencies of steel with coatings formed in various electrolytes (shown in Table 1). Upon reaching a potential of −330 mV, the passive film of the AISI 304 stainless steel is disrupted, leading to the formation of stable pits (Fig. S2). It should be noted that in the potential range from 0.2 V to the pitting potential, current density oscillations are observed, which are caused by successive processes of activation and subsequent passivation. This indicates the formation of metastable pitting on the steel surface [42,43]. Within the transpassivation region, reaching a potential of 200 mV, stainless steel shifts to an active state, leading to the generation of higher valence ions. The anode and cathode branches of the curves obtained for steel with black chromium coatings of various modifications exhibit a high degree of similarity. The black chromium coatings increase the passivity region by 565–667 ± 5 mV, indicating enhanced protective properties against pitting corrosion. Lowest passivity current density j pass = (1.34 ± 0.14)·10−6 A·cm−2 at a potential of 0 V has vs. SCE a coating modified with aluminum oxide. In this case, for Cr·CrOx and Cr·CrOx·TiOy coatings, the passivity current density has close values (3.89 ± 0.21)·10−6 and (5.49 ± 0.26)·10−6 A·cm−2 respectively. Apparently, this fact is due to the defectiveness of the coating, namely the presence or absence of cracks. As it was shown in Chapter 3.2. the Cr·CrOx·AlOz coating does not have cracks and therefore demonstrates higher protective properties.

The electrochemical parameter values are presented in Table 3 . It can be seen that the anodic Tafel slope (b a) for the modified coating with titanium oxide increased 9 times compared to uncoated steel and proves the effectiveness of the protective coating (shown in Table 3). The cathode Tafel slope (b с) decreased by 10 mV for the Cr·CrOx, by 30 mV for Cr·CrOx·TiOy and Cr·CrOx·AlOz coatings compared to steel. From the analysis of the results obtained, it follows that the coatings contain compounds in different oxidation states that are electrochemical active and participate in the electrochemical reaction.

In summary, the anodic and cathodic reactions governing the corrosion processes of steel in chloride solutions are influenced by reactions involving electrochemically active phases of metals in an intermediate oxidation state, as detailed in Chapter 3.2.

The Nyquist Bode and Fitted plots of the black chromium coatings on the AISI 304 stainless steel are depicted in Fig. 7 . On the Nyquist graphs of steel with black chromium coatings (shown in Fig. 7c, e, g), two overlapping semicircles are observed, with the initiation of another semicircle in the low-frequency region. Fig. 7b,d,f,h illustrates that the impedance modulus of black chromium coatings increases by 3–4 times compared to steel at a frequency of 1000 Hz, and for black chromium coatings modified with aluminum, this increase is 11 times, indicating a high protective property of these coatings. The phase shift angle versus frequency (shown in Fig. 7a) suggests that the electrical equivalent circuit for steel comprises one Constant Phase Element (CPE), while for black chromium coatings and those modified with titanium oxide, two CPEs are involved. For the coating modified with aluminum oxide, three CPEs are observed (shown in Fig. 8 ). The EEC for steel is relatively simple, consisting of electrolyte resistance (R s), charge transfer resistance (R 1), and the constant phase element (CPE1), all associated with corrosion processes at the AISI 304/natural oxide film/solution interface. The calculated values of the film thickness according to equation by Brug et al. [39] (Chapter 2.3) were <1 nm.

The EECs for black chromium coatings consist of multiple CPE circuits, reflecting the intricate structure of the coatings. For Cr·CrOx and Cr·CrOx·TiOy coatings, the circuits exhibit similar configurations. The elements in these circuits include R 1 and CPE1, representing the resistance and the constant phase element connected from the top layer of the coating; R 2 and CPE2, representing the resistance and the constant phase element connected with the cracked internal coating; W, representing the Warburg impedance representing the resistance and constant phase element associated with the processes occurring at the AISI 304/Cr·CrOx/solution or AISI 304/Cr·CrOx·TiOy/solution interface. It should be noted that the Warburg impedance was identified only for coatings with cracks. Moreover, for Cr·CrOx coatings, as was shown in Chapter 3.2, there is a large, branched network of wide cracks. Therefore, in this case, the spectrum fitting determined that the EEC includes the impedance of linear semi-infinite diffusion W о. For Cr·CrOx·TiOy coatings that have thin cracks, the fitting is described by the Warburg impedance W
s of a finite diffusion length (hyperbolic cotangent). W
s is usually used to describe a linear diffusion process occurring in a homogeneous layer of finite length, when one boundary has a fixed concentration of diffusing components. Thus, it can be assumed that the cracks in this coating are “non-through” in nature.

The circuit configuration differs for the coating AISI 304/Cr·CrOx·AlOz/solution, as it lacks a Warburg impedance and comprises three CPEs. The circuit includes R 1 and CPE1, representing the resistance and constant phase element of the top layer of the coating, while R 2 and CPE2 are linked to the bottom layer. Additionally, R 3 and CPE3 represent the resistance and CPE associated with the processes occurring at the AISI 304/Cr·CrOx·AlOz interface (shown in Table 4 ).

The Cr·CrOx coating exhibits the lowest R 1 value, attributed to its structural characteristics, notably the presence of significant cracks. The resistance values for Cr·CrOx·AlOz coatings are an order of magnitude greater than another cases, indicating a denser coating layer without cracks. The resistance values for both Cr·CrOx and Cr·CrOx·TiOy coatings is identical. The coating modified with aluminum oxide demonstrates the highest protective properties.

The effectiveness of the protective coatings on steel, operating as part of a galvanic couple with a zinc anode in a 6 wt% sodium chloride solution, was assessed by measuring the currents within the electrochemical couple [31,45–48]. The data shows that modifying with titanium or aluminum oxides significantly reduces the contact corrosion currents in the galvanic pair. Specifically, the current density decreased by 6–7.5 times after a 5-day exposure to a chloride solution, as detailed in Table 5 . The highest recorded current density value was observed in the contact between the zinc anode and stainless steel with a natural oxide film.

The coatings with black chromium underwent SEM analysis after contact corrosion testing. Fig. 9a-с present micrographs captured after the testing of coatings for contact corrosion, demonstrating the absence of corrosion foci and products. Although cracks were observed on samples of unmodified coatings before (Fig. S3) and after testing. XRD analysis after prolonged corrosion tests confirms the coatings resilience, as no significant structural degradation was observed (Fig. 5). Additionally, it is evident that the outward appearance of the modified coatings remained unchanged even after prolonged testing, underscoring their high level of protective properties.

The coatings with black chromium underwent SEM analysis after contact corrosion testing. Fig. 9a-с present micrographs captured after the testing of coatings for contact corrosion, demonstrating the absence of corrosion foci and products. Although cracks were observed on samples of unmodified coatings before (Fig. S3) and after testing. XRD analysis after prolonged corrosion tests confirms the coatings resilience, as no significant structural degradation was observed (Fig. 5). Additionally, it is evident that the outward appearance of the modified coatings remained unchanged even after prolonged testing, underscoring their high level of protective properties.

For all tested temperatures and isothermal holding times, there was an increase in the mass of steel specimens with and without coatings. All examined types of coatings exhibited similar behavior. The corrosion kinetics of the samples during 1 h of testing in a water vapor environment in the temperature range from 400 °C to 1000 °C showed a damped response (shown in Fig. 10a), indicating that the oxide films formed on them are protective [44,45].

Up to a test temperature of 800 °C, the rate of change in the mass of samples in a water vapor medium is low, which is typical for all austenitic chromium‑nickel steels [46]. At temperatures above 800 °C, a sharp increase in mass was observed (shown in Fig. 10a), a behavior characteristic of steels with this chemical composition. At a temperature of 800 °C for 1 h of testing, the mass gain was 220.0 mg·dm−2 for uncoated samples, 8.83 mg·dm−2 for samples coated with Cr·CrOx·AlOz, 10.9 mg·dm−2 for Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and 13.0 mg·dm−2 for Cr·CrOx.

Fig. 10b shows the experimental results of the change in the mass of samples during the first hour of corrosion testing at 800 °C in logarithmic coordinates. For each of the materials, the results obtained are superimposed on one segment, indicating that they can be described by a power equation. For AISI 304 steel, this is a section with a slope of 0.52, with a confidence probability of R2 = 0.98, which practically corresponds to the quadratic dependence of the mass change on the test time. For steel samples with Cr·CrOx, Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and Cr·CrOx·AlOz coatings, the pore coefficient is 0.35 (R2 = 0.98), 0.37 (R2 = 0.99), and 0.31 (R2 = 0.98), respectively.

A visual inspection of the sample surfaces after testing at 800 °C revealed that, on nearly the entire surface of the uncoated steel samples, a dark grey oxide film appeared, adorned with numerous small light grey oxide spots exhibiting varying densities across the surface. In certain regions, these oxide spots completely overlapped, creating areas with reduced reflectivity [49]. In contrast, the surfaces of all types of black chromium coatings remained largely unchanged, maintaining a dark matte film with low reflectivity.

A significant change in the appearance of the samples is observed after testing in water vapor at a temperature of 900 °C. The surface of uncoated steel samples becomes uneven, loses its reflectivity, acquires a dark brown color with local black areas [50]. The surface of coated samples also becomes loose, but their color does not change. The friability of the surface indicates peeling of the surface layers of the protective coating, but the loss of mass is almost not recorded. The change in the mass of samples after the test for one hour at a temperature of 900 °C is 450 mg·dm−2 for uncoated samples and 120 mg·dm−2 for Cr·CrOx, 110 mg·dm−2 for Cr·CrOx·TiOy, and 100 mg·dm−2 for Cr·CrOx·AlOz.

After one hour of testing at a temperature of 1000 °C, the disparity in the mass change of the samples diminishes (shown in Fig. 11 ). In both coated and uncoated steels, the surface layers of the oxide film are eradicated. The weight gain for the samples is 850 mg·dm−2 and 593 mg·dm−2 for the uncoated and black chromium-coated samples with any modification, respectively. Notably, the coated samples experience intense shedding.

It has been observed that black chromium coatings exhibit a crack-like structure, and the incorporation of titanium and aluminum compounds reduces both the quantity and width of these cracks. Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis results have confirmed the presence of titanium and aluminum elements in the coating composition. X-ray diffraction analysis has established that the structure of the obtained coatings is crystalline, with the presence of complex oxides of valve metals confirmed.

The increase in the passivity area due to the modification of coatings with black chromium has been identified, indicating high protective properties against pitting corrosion. The impedance spectroscopy method was employed to study the properties of the resulting coatings. It was demonstrated that the inclusion of aluminum compounds in black chromium leads to a substantial increase in the coating's durability in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

The corrosion test data obtained for contact corrosion in a 6 wt% NaCl solution indicate that modification with titanium and aluminum oxides decreases the rate of zinc corrosion by 6 times and 7.5 times, respectively, thereby enhancing the protective effect. Long-term tests of modified coatings in the operation of a galvanic pair with zinc are consistent with the results obtained from impedance spectroscopy.

The corrosion resistance of AISI 304 steel specimens with a black chromium coating at 800 °C is 20 times higher compared to uncoated steel specimens, as evidenced by their weight gain. Samples with black chromium coating modified by aluminum oxides exhibited the lowest weight loss. However, at a temperature of 900 °C, this difference decreases to 4.1 times, indicating a reduction in the protective properties of the black chromium coating. Moreover, shedding at temperatures above 900 °C suggests a loss of adhesion to the surface of the base metal.

Thus, the modification of coatings with aluminum oxides enhances crystallinity, which is directly linked to improved corrosion resistance. On the other hand, coatings modified with titanium oxides exhibit lower crystallinity and, consequently, reduced corrosion resistance. These conclusions are supported by both XRD data and the results of corrosion tests, which confirm that coatings with a high degree of crystallinity provide more effective protection against corrosion.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
SEM image of the Cr·CrOx black chrome coating on AISI 304 stainless steel.
Fig. S1
Fig. S2
SEM image of the AISI 304 steel surface after polarization tests.
Fig. S2
Fig. S3
Optical micrograph of the Cr·CrOx black chrome coating on AISI 304 stainless steel before the contact corrosion test.
Fig. S3
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.surfcoat.2024.131706.

Viktoriia Shtefan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Nadiia Kanunnikova: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Valeriy Zuyok: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
